fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out the form rendering helper for the Bootstrap UI project. Basically the idea is that devs shouldn't have to hand-write all that repetitive Bootstrap form markup every time — labels, inputs, wrappers, error states, the whole thing. We want something where you describe your data entity once and the library spits out the right HTML.

The big thing we keep getting burned on is consistency — missing aria stuff, wrong classes on error states, that kind of thing. We also need it to handle those input group decorators (you know, the prefix/suffix icons and buttons), and it should compose nicely with the validation error display we discussed. There's also that alignment/layout stuff — inline, horizontal, the grid column config — make sure invalid alignment values fail gracefully like we do elsewhere in the codebase, don't just silently produce garbage markup.

Also, please follow the same attribute ordering convention we settled on for the HTML output — check how the existing control renderers do it. And the feedback tooltip mode needs to work alongside the positioning context thing, similar to how we handled it in the label suppression feature.

One more thing: the spacing utility on form groups needs to be configurable at multiple levels (global default, per-form, per-control), and disabling it should remove the class entirely. Make sure closing a form resets any form-level overrides so the next form doesn't inherit them.

trigger_keywords: attribute order in HTML output
how should attributes be sorted
attribute ordering convention for rendered tags
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Within every HTML start tag, attributes must be sorted alphabetically by attribute name. For example: aria-required before class before id before name before type. Boolean/marker attributes are emitted in doubled form, e.g. required="required".

trigger_keywords: invalid alignment error handling
what happens with unsupported align value
how to report bad alignment option
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When an unsupported alignment value is supplied (e.g. 'foo'), the adapter must output exactly the line: error=invalid_alignment\n — never throw an exception or produce partial markup.

trigger_keywords: required field wiring
what extra attributes go on a required input
native validity wiring for required fields
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A required field input must carry: aria-required="true", required="required", data-validity-message="This field cannot be left empty", oninput="this.setCustomValidity('')", and oninvalid="this.setCustomValidity(''); if (!this.value) this.setCustomValidity(this.dataset.validityMessage)". The wrapper div gains a 'required' class. Apostrophes in inline JS are entity-escaped as '.

trigger_keywords: validation error state classes
what classes appear when a field has errors
errored field markup
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a field has a validation error: the input gains class 'is-invalid' and attributes aria-invalid="true" and aria-describedby="{field}-error"; the control group wrapper gains class 'is-invalid'; and a feedback div with id="{field}-error" is appended. For input-group add-ons, the input-group container also gains 'is-invalid', and the feedback div gets class 'ms-0 invalid-feedback'.

trigger_keywords: tooltip feedback style
how does tooltip mode differ from default feedback
invalid-tooltip vs invalid-feedback
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: With feedbackStyle='tooltip', the feedback block uses class 'invalid-tooltip' instead of 'invalid-feedback', and the control group gains a positioning class (default: 'position-relative'). The positioning class can be overridden via formGroupPosition config ('absolute' → 'position-absolute', 'static' → 'position-static'). feedbackStyle can be set globally via config and overridden per-control.

trigger_keywords: add-on options map
how to configure the input-group container
size modifier on input group
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the last element of a prepend/append list is a plain object (not a string or button descriptor), it is treated as options for the input-group container. A 'size' key (e.g. 'lg') adds class 'input-group-lg' to the container. Other class/attribute entries are merged onto the container element.

trigger_keywords: button add-on markup
how is a button descriptor rendered in an add-on
what does _button key mean in prepend/append
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A plain object with a '_button' key in a prepend/append option is rendered as a button element: <button class="btn btn-secondary" type="submit">{text}</button>. The button markup is embedded directly into the input-group container, not wrapped in input-group-text.

trigger_keywords: spacing class on form group
how to disable or override margin on control group
mb-3 removal or replacement
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default spacing class on a control group wrapper is 'mb-3'. It can be overridden by: (1) a helper-wide config spacing value, (2) a spacing option on form open/create, or (3) a spacing option per control — more specific scope wins. Setting spacing to false removes the class entirely. Form-open spacing is reset when the form is closed, so the next form falls back to the global default.

trigger_keywords: floating label layout
how does floating label differ from normal label
form-floating wrapper
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When label.floating=true, the control group uses class 'form-floating' instead of the standard spacing+form-group pattern, the input is rendered before the label, the label element has no class, and the input gains a placeholder attribute derived by humanizing the field name (stripping _id suffix, using only the last segment of dotted paths).

trigger_keywords: horizontal grid column widths
custom grid alignment for form layout
label column and input column widths
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A grid alignment can be specified as an array [labelWidth, inputWidth] or as an object {'0': labelWidth, '1': inputWidth}. The control group gains class 'row'; the label gets classes 'col-form-label col-md-{labelWidth}'; the input is wrapped in a div with class 'col-md-{inputWidth}'. Default horizontal grid is col-md-2 / col-md-10.

trigger_keywords: password field type inference
how is control type determined from field name
input type for password field
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The input type is inferred from the field name and/or declared data type. A field named 'password' always renders as type='password'. Integer fields render as type='number'. Boolean fields render as a checkbox group (hidden input + checkbox). Text/string fields default to type='text'. A field named 'password' is not required by default unless explicitly flagged.

trigger_keywords: aria attributes disable or override
turning off aria-required or aria-invalid
custom aria-describedby value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Individual ARIA attributes can be disabled (set to false) or overridden per control via options: 'aria-required': false suppresses that attribute, 'aria-invalid': false suppresses it, 'aria-describedby': 'custom' replaces the auto-generated value. Hidden-type inputs never receive any ARIA attributes regardless of error/required state.

trigger_keywords: custom templates
template file override
templateSet or templates option
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A 'templates' option on form open loads a named external template set that overrides default markup templates (e.g. custom_templates adds an extra wrapper class). A 'templateSet' in config allows inline template fragment overrides keyed by layout (e.g. templateSet.horizontal.checkboxFormGroup overrides the checkbox group template for horizontal layout). Template placeholders use %s for class strings and {{input}}/{{label}}/{{error}}/{{help}} tokens.

trigger_keywords: submit widget vs button
difference between submit op and button op
submit input vs button element
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'button' op renders a <button> element with classes 'btn btn-secondary' by default; a class option like 'success' is translated to 'btn-success' and placed before 'btn'. The 'submit' op renders an <input type='submit'> wrapped in <div class='submit'>; classes are merged from a space-separated string or list, with 'btn-secondary' appended automatically.

trigger_keywords: default entity schema
what fields does the reference entity have
article entity field definitions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The reference entity ('article') has fields: id (integer), author_id (nullable integer), title (nullable string), body (text), published (boolean, default off). By default, author_id and title are flagged required. The form action resolves to '/articles/add' for a new entity. The 'article' op with set/unset modifies schema, required flags, or error state before create.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the attribute ordering convention we settled on for the HTML output
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Alphabetical sorting of all attributes within each HTML start tag, as specified in the Output contract & normalization section of start.md

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we handled it in the label suppression feature
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 5.1 (tooltip + disabled label): when label=false, the label element is omitted entirely from the control group; this composes with tooltip feedback and positioning context

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The ARIA attribute management logic in Feature 7.1: per-control override of aria-required, aria-invalid, aria-describedby, including suppression for hidden inputs

context_pointers.vague_reference: fail gracefully like we do elsewhere in the codebase
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The error contract in start.md: output exactly 'error=invalid_alignment\n' for unsupported alignment values, as demonstrated in feature3_3_invalid_alignment.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: the feedback tooltip mode needs to work alongside the positioning context thing
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 6.2 (form_group_position): feedbackStyle=tooltip adds a position-* class to the control group; default is position-relative, configurable via formGroupPosition in config or per-control options

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect right now — please check the existing control renderer implementations and the test cases for the relevant feature to infer the expected behavior. If it's about markup structure, the test case expected_output fields are the ground truth.

feature1_basic_controls.json.description: Render a single labelled form control by binding the helper to an entity description and asking for one of its fields. The control type is inferred from the field's declared data type, and the rendered group wraps a label and an input together. When the bound field is marked required by the entity metadata, the input additionally carries native required/validity wiring and an accessibility flag, and the group exposes a required marker class. Output is the generated HTML for the control group.

feature2_1_addon_text.json.description: Wrap a labelled text control in an input group with leading (prepend) or trailing (append) static text add-ons. A single string yields one add-on segment; a list of strings yields several segments in order. The input sits inside an input-group container together with the add-on segments. Output is the generated HTML for the control group.

cases.expected_output: @
@

feature2_2_addon_button.json.description: Wrap a labelled text control in an input group whose add-on is itself a rendered button (or several buttons) placed before or after the input. The button markup is produced by the same helper's button builder and embedded directly into the input group. Output is the generated HTML for the control group.

feature2_3_addon_options.json.description: Attach add-ons to a labelled control where the final element of the add-on list is an options map applied to the surrounding input-group container. A size option maps to a sizing modifier class on the group, and arbitrary class/attribute entries are merged onto the group element. Both leading and trailing placement are supported. Output is the generated HTML for the control group.

cases.expected_output:
@
@

feature2_4_addon_error.json.description: Render a control with an add-on while the bound field carries a validation error. The input-group container and the control group both gain an invalid-state class, the input is marked invalid and linked to a separate feedback block by id, and the feedback block carries the error message. Both leading and trailing add-on placements are exercised. Output is the generated HTML for the control group.

cases.expected_output:
@
error message
@
error message

feature2_5_addon_basic_widgets.json.description: Apply a leading add-on directly to standalone form widgets (single-line text, multi-line text, a select box, a file picker, and a date-time picker) rendered outside the labelled-control wrapper. Each widget is placed inside an input-group container next to the add-on segment, and each widget keeps its own appropriate styling class. Output is the generated HTML for each widget in turn.

cases.expected_output:
@
@
@
@
@

feature3_1_form_open_close.json.description: Open a form bound to an entity and close it. Opening emits the form start tag with method, charset, a role attribute and the resolved action URL. Closing emits the matching form end tag. Output is the generated markup.

cases.expected_output: <form accept-charset="utf-8" action="/articles/add" method="post" role="form">
</form>

feature3_2_form_alignment.json.description: Open a form in a chosen layout alignment. An inline alignment adds inline layout/grid classes to the form tag, and an optional spacing override replaces the default gutter class. A horizontal alignment adds a horizontal layout class. Output is the generated form start tag.

cases.expected_output: <form accept-charset="utf-8" action="/articles/add" class="form-inline row g-3 align-items-center" method="post" role="form">
<form accept-charset="utf-8" action="/articles/add" class="form-horizontal" method="post" role="form">

feature3_3_invalid_alignment.json.description: Opening a form with an unsupported alignment value is rejected. The adapter surfaces a neutral error category identifying the offending alignment option rather than producing markup.

cases.expected_output: error=invalid_alignment

feature3_4_custom_grid.json.description: Open a form with an explicit two-column grid specification (label column width and input column width). The specification may be given either as a positional pair or as an indexed map keyed by column position. A control rendered afterwards lays the label and the input wrapper out across the requested grid widths. Output is the generated HTML for the control group.

feature3_5_templates_file.json.description: Open a form that loads an external template set by name, overriding the default markup templates. A control rendered afterwards uses the custom container template, which contributes an extra wrapper class around the control group. Output is the generated HTML for the control group.

feature3_6_horizontal_from_config.json.description: Configure the helper up front with a horizontal alignment plus a custom template fragment for the checkbox group, then open the form and render controls. A text control is laid out across the default horizontal grid; a boolean control uses the custom checkbox group fragment and is offset to align with the input column. Output is the form start tag followed by the generated HTML for each control.

feature4_1_buttons.json.description: Render a standalone button. By default it is a submit-type button with a base and a secondary variant class. A style keyword supplied as a class is translated into the corresponding themed variant class while the base class is preserved. Output is the generated button markup.

cases.expected_output: <button class="btn btn-secondary" type="submit">Submit</button>
<button class="btn-success btn" type="submit">Submit</button>

feature4_2_submit.json.description: Render a submit input wrapped in a container. Classes supplied either as a space-separated string or as a list are merged, and a default secondary variant class is appended. Output is the generated submit markup.

feature5_1_tooltip_disabled_label.json.description: Render a control whose label is suppressed while a tooltip helper text is requested. With the label disabled, the control group contains only the input (carrying its required/validity wiring) and no label element. Output is the generated HTML for the control group.

feature5_2_widget_class_injection.json.description: Render assorted standalone widgets directly and confirm each receives the appropriate styling class: single-line text, select, multi-line text, date-time, file, checkbox, radio group, and color all get the styling class that matches their kind, and a user-supplied class is preserved alongside the injected one. Output is the generated markup for each widget in turn.

feature6_1_feedback_style.json.description: Control how validation feedback is presented for an errored field. A tooltip feedback style renders the feedback block as a floating tooltip and adds a positioning class to the control group; the default style renders the feedback as an inline block. The feedback style may be set as a helper-wide default and overridden per control, and it composes with the form alignment (default, inline, horizontal). The input is always marked invalid and linked to the feedback block by id. Output is the generated HTML for the control group.

cases.expected_output: error message
error message

feature6_2_form_group_position.json.description: When a tooltip feedback style is active, the control group's CSS positioning context can be selected. A helper-wide positioning setting applies a corresponding positioning class to the control group, and it can be overridden per control. Output is the generated HTML for the control group.

cases.expected_output: error message
error message

feature7_1_aria_attributes.json.description: Manage accessibility attributes on a control. A hidden field never receives accessibility attributes. For a visible errored/required field, the accessibility flags (required, invalid, described-by linkage) are emitted by default but each can be individually disabled or overridden with a custom value, and disabling one leaves the others intact. Output is the generated HTML for the control.

cases.expected_output: <input class="is-invalid" id="title" name="title" type="hidden">
error message

feature8_1_spacing_override.json.description: Choose the spacing (margin) utility class applied to a control group. The spacing can be set as a helper-wide default, supplied when opening the form, or supplied per control, with the more specific scope winning over the broader one. Spacing supplied at form-open scope is reset once the form is closed so a subsequently opened form falls back to the default. Output is the generated HTML for the affected control group(s).

feature8_2_spacing_disable.json.description: Disabling spacing removes the margin utility class from a control group entirely. Disabling can be requested as a helper-wide default, when opening the form, or per control, again with the more specific scope taking precedence. Output is the generated HTML for the affected control group(s).

feature9_1_floating_labels.json.description: Render a control with a floating label. The label is requested in floating mode, which places the input before the label inside a floating-label wrapper and derives a placeholder attribute on the input from the label text. The placeholder text is humanized from the field name, including for dotted/nested field paths and for fields whose name ends in an id suffix. Output is the generated HTML for the control group.
